chapter_summary: This prompt bundles two distinct chapter-openings. (A) Bhagavad Gita, Chapter 1 (Arjuna-vishada / 'survey of the armies', verses 1-15): the blind king Dhritarashtra asks his charioteer-narrator Sanjaya what his sons (Kauravas) and the Pandavas did on the sacred field of Kurukshetra (dharmakshetra). Sanjaya reports that Duryodhana, seeing the Pandava army drawn up in battle-formation by Dhrishtadyumna (son of Drupada, disciple of Drona), approaches his teacher Drona and recites the great warriors on both sides — the Pandava champions (Bhima, Arjuna, Yuyudhana/Satyaki, Virata, Drupada, Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, Kashiraja, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, Shaibya, Yudhamanyu, Uttamaujas, the sons of Subhadra and of Draupadi) and his own (Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, the son of Somadatta), urging all to protect Bhishma. Bhishma, the Kuru grandsire, blows his conch like a lion's roar; war-drums and horns erupt. Then Krishna (Madhava/Hrishikesha) and Arjuna (Pandava/Dhananjaya), in a chariot drawn by white horses, blow their divine conches Panchajanya and Devadatta, joined by Bhima (Vrikodara) with his great conch Paundra. The fragment breaks off mid-verse 15. (B) Gheranda Samhita, Chapter 1 (verses 0-22), framed as a dialogue: the seeker Canda Kapali approaches the sage Gheranda and asks to be taught Ghatastha Yoga (the yoga of the body-vessel) as the cause of knowledge of reality (tattvajnana). Gheranda praises the question and declares that there is no bondage like maya, no power greater than yoga, no friend higher than knowledge, and no enemy worse than ego. He explains that the jiva, like a potter's wheel turning by the force of past good and bad deeds (karma), revolves through birth and death; the body is an unbaked clay pot that must be fired in the flame of yoga to be purified. He then lays out the sevenfold discipline of Ghatastha Yoga — purification, firmness, steadiness, patience, lightness, direct perception, and non-attachment — mapped onto seven limbs (shatkarma, asana, mudra, pratyahara, pranayama, dhyana, samadhi) culminating in liberation. The chapter then begins the shatkarmas, detailing the four kinds of dhauti (cleansing), especially the fourfold antardhauti (vatasara air-cleansing, varisara water-cleansing, vahnisara fire/navel-cleansing, and bahishkrita), promising that mastery yields a "divine body" (devadeha) and freedom from disease — secret practices not to be revealed.

story.title: The Conch-Blast at Kurukshetra and the Potter's Wheel: Two Openings of War, Outer and Inner
story.arc: Two chapter-openings set side by side trace one movement, from the battlefield without to the battlefield within. In the Gita fragment, a blind king's anxious question pulls the listener onto the field of Kurukshetra where two kin-armies face off; Duryodhana's nervous roll-call of heroes to his own teacher Drona betrays the dread beneath his bravado, Bhishma's lion-roar conch answers it, and then Krishna and Arjuna's divine conches — Panchajanya and Devadatta — cut through with the cosmic note that announces the real war about to be named. In the Gheranda Samhita fragment the battlefield turns inward: the seeker Canda Kapali travels to the sage Gheranda, bows in devotion, and asks how to know reality. Gheranda answers that the body itself is the contested field — the embodied soul spun helplessly on the potter's wheel of its own karma, an unbaked clay pot soaking in water and ever decaying, useless until it is fired hard in the flame of yoga. From this image he derives the whole path: a sevenfold discipline realized through seven limbs that ends without doubt in liberation, then descends into the concrete craft of the shatkarmas and the four internal cleansings of air, water, and fire — each guarded as a secret mystery, each promising the mortal pot becomes a divine, deathless body.
story.timeline_note: The Gita fragment (Mahabharata, Bhishma Parva) sits at the very start of the Kurukshetra war, moments before battle and before Krishna speaks the Gita proper; the surveyed armies and conch-blasts are pre-discourse. The Gheranda Samhita is a late-medieval hatha-yoga manual (c. 17th century), acausal and instructional rather than narrative-historical: its frame is a single uninterrupted teaching session ('ekada' — 'once'), opening with an invocatory salutation (GhS_1.0) before 'once, Canda Kapali went...' (GhS_1.1), and the shown text ends mid-flow within the dhauti section (GhS_1.22). The two chapters are unrelated in time and tradition; they share only this prompt.
story.comic_potential: On the Gita side, Duryodhana praising the enemy army to his own teacher and then anxiously over-counting his own side is unintentionally comic — the bully doing nervous math. On the yoga side, the deadpan gravity of cleansing instructions ('drink air slowly like a crow's beak, churn the belly, expel it downward') described as the gateway to cosmic liberation, plus the earnest secrecy ('this is supremely secret, never to be revealed') wrapped around an elaborate internal-plumbing routine, has a dry guru-infomercial humor. The soul as a hapless clay pot spun on a cow-driven potter's wheel is begging for an animated visual gag.

teachings.teaching: The field of action is named dharmakshetra-kurukshetra, the 'field of dharma', framing the coming war as a moral and spiritual arena rather than mere slaughter; the very first word sets the contest in terms of righteousness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framing, Kurukshetra is the body and its dharma-field is the inner battleground of consciousness; the war is the sadhaka's confrontation with his own conditioned tendencies (the kindred Kauravas), fought along the spine and breath. The blind king who can only ask 'what did they do?' is the ego-mind that cannot itself see the field and must rely on a reporting witness (Sanjaya / the awakened attention).

teachings.teaching: Before any discourse of wisdom, the divine conches Panchajanya and Devadatta sound from the chariot of white horses — primordial sound (nada) precedes and announces the teaching.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the conch's nada prefigures the inner sound (anahata-nada / omkara) the yogi hears when prana is gathered and stilled; the outer blast mirrors the awakening of the inner resonance that announces the real Gita — the dialogue of the higher Self (Krishna, the charioteer who holds the reins of the senses, Hrishikesha) with the striving self (Arjuna). The chariot of white horses is the purified body-vehicle yoked to the disciplined senses.

teachings.teaching: There is no bondage equal to maya, no power greater than yoga, no friend higher than knowledge (jnana), and no enemy worse than ego (ahamkara).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework this is the entire map in one verse: ahamkara — the false 'I' rooted in the lower centers — is the single knot that maya tightens, and the only real adversary on the path. Yoga is named 'the greatest power' because, unlike philosophy, it is a kriya — an applied technology that actually severs the knot rather than describing it. Knowledge (jnana) here is not bookish information but the direct experiential gnosis (tattvajnana) that dawns when prana is mastered; it is the 'friend' because it is what survives when the ego-pot is finally fired away.

teachings.teaching: The embodied soul (jiva) revolves through birth and death by the force of its karma, exactly as a potter's wheel is spun up and down by the ox that drives it; good and bad deeds together produce the body, and the body in turn generates fresh karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the potter's-wheel (ghatayantra) image as the mechanics of the karmic vritti-cycle: as long as prana flows outward and downward through ordinary breathing, the wheel keeps turning and the jiva is passive cargo on it. Kriya's purpose is to redirect that very pranic force inward and upward (the reversal of the 'wheel') so the soul stops being spun and becomes the still axle — the witness at the center. The body is not condemned; it is precisely the instrument through which the cycle is broken, because the wheel can only be stopped from within the apparatus itself.

teachings.teaching: The body is an unbaked clay pot soaking in water, always decaying; it must be fired and hardened in the flame of yoga (yogagni) to achieve the purification of the vessel (ghata-shuddhi).
teachings.lens_note: This is the literal charter of Kriya as Sharma teaches it: the 'fire of yoga' (yoganala) is the awakened inner fire — the heat generated when prana is concentrated and the jatharagni / kundalini-fire is kindled by sustained practice. An unbaked pot dissolves back into mud (re-death); a fired pot holds its form and can hold the nectar. Sharma insists the body must be deliberately cooked by disciplined kriya before any higher state is stable — purification is not a metaphor for moral effort but a real physiological transmutation that makes the body a fit, non-leaking container for consciousness (the 'devadeha').

teachings.teaching: Ghatastha Yoga is a sevenfold discipline: purification (shodhana), firmness (dridhata), steadiness (sthairya), patience (dhairya), lightness (laghava), direct perception (pratyaksha) and non-attachment (nirlipta) — attained respectively through shatkarma, asana, mudra, pratyahara, pranayama, dhyana, and samadhi — and it culminates beyond doubt in liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma treats this seven-rung ladder as the engineered sequence Kriya follows in practice, not an arbitrary list: you cannot leap to dhyana while the vessel still leaks. Purification (shatkarma) clears the channels; asana stabilizes the body so prana can settle; mudra and pranayama work the energy directly; only on that prepared ground do dhyana (which yields pratyaksha — direct seeing of the Atman, not belief) and samadhi (which yields nirlipta — the unsmeared, witness state) become possible. The promise 'muktir eva na samsayah' — liberation without doubt — is Sharma's recurring claim that yoga is an exact science with a guaranteed result when the steps are honored in order.

teachings.teaching: Through the dhauti cleansings — especially the fourfold internal antardhauti of air (vatasara), water (varisara), fire/navel (vahnisara) and expulsion (bahishkrita) — the body is made spotless, the gastric fire is increased, all diseases are destroyed, and one who perfects them attains a divine body; these practices are supremely secret and must never be casually revealed.
teachings.lens_note: Here the lens and the source agree completely with Sharma's lived emphasis: the shatkarmas are not hygiene for its own sake but the kindling of the inner fire (dehanala / jatharagni) that the whole edifice of Kriya depends on — a strong inner fire is what burns the karmic residue and dries out the 'soaking pot.' The insistence on secrecy ('para gopya, na prakasya kadacana') is the textual anchor for the live system's Practice-and-Initiation guardrail: these are not self-study techniques but transmissions that belong to the direct Guru-disciple relationship. The 'devadeha' (divine body) is Sharma's goal of a transmuted, deathless-disposed physical vehicle — the fired pot that can finally hold the divine.
